reason: The remediation involves setting strict permissions (0700) on /etc/cron.weekly and removing special bits (setuid, setgid, sticky). This directory contains system cron jobs executed by root. Changing permissions to restrict access to root only is a standard hardening measure with minimal risk. It does not affect boot, authentication, networking, or filesystem structure. The worst-case impact is that non-root users lose access to view or modify these cron jobs, which is intentional and expected.
estimated_impact: If custom cron jobs rely on non-root users having read/execute access to /etc/cron.weekly (highly unusual and against best practices), those jobs may fail to run. However, system cron directories should be root-owned and restricted per security guidelines.; Incorrect execution of the remediation command (e.g., typos) could affect other paths, but the provided script is scoped to the specific directory.
